note: Curated vessel fun facts for the banner notch, researched + adversarially fact-checked. `vessels` keyed by name (case-insensitive). `general` = Straits of Mackinac shipping facts used as a last-resort fallback when a vessel has no fact and no AIS size/type.

vessels.name: PAUL R TREGURTHA
vessels.fact: Queen of the Lakes — longest ship on the Great Lakes, 1,013 ft
vessels.source: interlake-steamship.com (1013.5 ft, Queen of the Lakes since 1981)

vessels.name: VIKING OCTANTIS
vessels.fact: Largest cruise ship sailing the Great Lakes — 665 ft
vessels.source: seatrade-cruise.com / urbanmilwaukee.com 2025 (polar-rating claim dropped, unverified; sources vary 665-673 ft)

vessels.name: LEO A.MCARTHUR
vessels.fact: Tug that pushes a heated liquid-asphalt barge year-round
vessels.source: gltugs.wordpress.com/leo-a-mcarthur (built 2009 as Victorious, renamed 2017; mated to heated asphalt barge John J. Carrick, year-round). Length 122 ft not confirmed — zeroed.

vessels.name: FLORENCE SPIRIT
vessels.fact: Collided head-on in the Welland Canal in 2020 — both survived
vessels.source: TSB Canada report M20C0188 (11 July 2020 collision with Alanis). Corrected: it was an actual head-on collision, not a 'near' miss; 'went viral' unverified and removed. Length 447 ft not confirmed — zeroed.

vessels.name: ALGOMA NIAGARA
vessels.fact: First Equinox-class self-unloader built for Algoma
vessels.source: drybulkmagazine.com / businesswire.com 2017 (first Equinox-class self-unloader; earlier Equinox ships were gearless). Length 740 ft not confirmed — zeroed.

vessels.name: OLIVE L. MOORE
vessels.fact: Built 1928 — still pushing barges after 97 years
vessels.source: boatnerd.com / masoncountypress.com May 2025 (launched 16 Apr 1928 as John F. Cushing; confirmed still working with barge Menominee)

vessels.name: HON JAMES L OBERSTAR
vessels.fact: Built 1959, stretched 96 ft in 1972 — now 806 ft long
vessels.source: boatnerd.com / interlake-steamship.com (built 1959 as Shenango II, lengthened 96 ft at Fraser Shipyards 1972, now 806.0 ft)

vessels.name: STERLING ENERGY
vessels.fact: A floating gas station — refuels other ships in port
vessels.source: sterlingfuels.ca / seawayreview.com / vesselfinder IMO 9277058 (bunkering tanker, built 2002, 64 m = 210 ft; corrected from 213 ft). Upgraded from medium to high.

vessels.name: ALGOMA DISCOVERY
vessels.fact: Gearless bulk carrier built 1987 — no self-unloading boom
vessels.source: Wikipedia Algoma Central fleet list (1987, gearless bulk carrier, in service). Croatian-build claim could NOT be verified and was removed. Length 729 ft not confirmed — zeroed.

vessels.name: EEMSBORG
vessels.fact: Dutch-flagged 'salty' — an ocean ship visiting the Lakes
vessels.source: vesselfinder IMO 9423748 (general cargo ship, built 2009, Netherlands flag, 137.84 m = 452 ft; corrected from 453 ft)

vessels.name: EDWIN H. GOTT
vessels.fact: 1,004 ft — longest self-unloading boom on the Great Lakes
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MV_Edwin_H._Gott

vessels.name: FRONTENAC
vessels.fact: Helped search for the Edmund Fitzgerald in 1975
vessels.source: greatlakesships.wordpress.com/frontenac/ · cslships.com/vessel/frontenac

vessels.name: LEE A. TREGURTHA
vessels.fact: WWII Navy oiler USS Chiwawa — earned two battle stars
vessels.source: interlake-steamship.com · en.wikipedia.org/wiki/USS_Chiwawa

vessels.name: PERELIK
vessels.fact: Bulgarian-run saltie named for the Rhodopes' highest peak
vessels.source: vesselfinder.com IMO 9905722 · en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Golyam_Perelik

vessels.name: SEA EAGLE II
vessels.fact: 7,200 hp pusher tug coupled into a cement barge as one unit
vessels.source: gltugs.wordpress.com/sea-eagle-ii/

vessels.name: STORMONT
vessels.fact: Built 1953 — still pushing barges after 70+ years
vessels.source: mckeil.com/our-fleet/stormont/ · gltugs.wordpress.com/stormont/

vessels.name: MACCOA
vessels.fact: Canfornav names its whole fleet after ducks — this one too
vessels.source: canfornav.com/fleet · vesselfinder.com IMO 9413913

vessels.name: OAKGLEN
vessels.fact: 730 ft Seaway-max bulker in CSL's 'glen' fleet since 1980
vessels.source: cslships.com/vessel/oakglen

vessels.name: SAMUEL RISLEY
vessels.fact: Icebreaker that can chew through 2 ft of Great Lakes ice
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CCGS_Samuel_Risley

vessels.name: IVER BRIGHT
vessels.fact: Dutch-flag bitumen tanker hauling hot liquid asphalt
vessels.source: vesselfinder.com IMO 9616759

vessels.name: MCASPHALT ADVANTAGE
vessels.fact: New in 2024 — keeps its cargo heated so it stays liquid
vessels.source: shipfax.blogspot.com/2025/09/mcasphalt-advantage-first-timer.html

vessels.name: ALGOMA ENDEAVOUR
vessels.fact: Delivered 2025 — one of Algoma's newest Seaway-max lakers
vessels.source: algonet.com/ship/algoma-endeavour/

vessels.name: FEDERAL SAKURA
vessels.fact: Japanese-built 2005 saltie sailing for Montreal's Fednav
vessels.source: vesselfinder.com IMO 9288291 — built Shin Kurushima Onishi, Japan; owner Fednav, Montreal; LOA 190.44 m

vessels.name: JAMES J. VERSLUIS
vessels.fact: Chicago's ice-crushing tug — smashing lake ice since 1957
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/James_J._Versluis; WBEZ Chicago — 90 ft, built 1957 Sturgeon Bay, breaks 18 in of ice

vessels.name: MUNTGRACHT
vessels.fact: Spliethoff cargo ship · 3 cranes, biggest lifting 80 tons
vessels.source: scheepvaartwest.be IMO 9571545 — cranes 80/65/50 t, LOA 142.1 m. CORRECTED: built 2009 at Zhoushan, China, not 2012

vessels.name: DETROIT PRINCESS
vessels.fact: Ex-casino boat from 1993 Louisiana · carries 1,900 guests
vessels.source: detroitprincess.com/page-about-1.php. CORRECTED: NOT a paddlewheeler (two Cummins diesels); deck count not supported by official source

vessels.name: J.S.ST.JOHN
vessels.fact: Built 1945 as US Navy oiler YO-178 · now a sand dredger
vessels.source: greatlakes.bgsu.edu/item/441100 — built 1945 Pensacola FL as USS YO-178, converted to sandsucker 1951, renamed J.S. St. John 1967; 51 m

vessels.name: BURNS HARBOR
vessels.fact: 1,000 ft long · hauls 80,900 tons of coal or iron ore
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MV_Burns_Harbor. CORRECTED: 80,900 dwt at midsummer draft; the '78,850 tons in seven holds' claim was not supported

vessels.name: INDIANA HARBOR
vessels.fact: 1,000-ft self-unloader built 1979 in Sturgeon Bay, Wisconsin
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MV_Indiana_Harbor. CORRECTED: 250-ft swinging boom claim unverifiable; replaced with confirmed build details

vessels.name: JOHN J. BOLAND
vessels.fact: Sailed as Charles E. Wilson until 2000 · 680 ft self-unloader
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MV_John_J._Boland — launched Mar 1973 as Charles E. Wilson, renamed Jan 2000; 680 ft; American Steamship Co

vessels.name: ROBERT S. PIERSON
vessels.fact: Sailed as Wolverine for 34 years · renamed Pierson in 2008
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MV_Robert_S._Pierson. CORRECTED: launched Sept 1973 (maiden voyage 1974); 630 ft, not unknown

vessels.name: HERBERT C JACKSON
vessels.fact: Steam-powered for 57 years — went diesel in winter 2015-16
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SS_Alpena (documents the winter 2015-16 conversion of Herbert C. Jackson and John G. Munson to diesel); VesselFinder (built 1959). 'Interlake's last steamer' could not be confirmed and was dropped.

vessels.name: GARDNO
vessels.fact: Built 2018 — 656 ft carrying 36,600 tons of cargo
vessels.source: VesselFinder IMO 9767704 (200 m LOA, 36,643 DWT, built 2018). Polsteam ownership and the '3 paying passengers' claim could NOT be verified — Polsteam's Wikipedia fleet list does not include Gardno and the flag is Bahamas; both dropped.

vessels.name: ALGOMA EQUINOX
vessels.fact: Lead ship of the Equinox class — 740 ft, built 2013 in China
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Algoma_Equinox — confirms lead ship of her class, 225.6 m (740 ft 2 in), completed 25 Sep 2013 at Nantong Mingde Heavy Industries, China

vessels.name: ALGOMA COMPASS
vessels.fact: First ship built in Toledo since 1959 — sailing since 1973
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Algoma_Compass — explicitly states as Roger M. Kyes she 'marked the first ship to be constructed in Toledo since 1959'; launched 28 Mar 1973, completed 1 Aug 1973, 207.3 m

vessels.name: LAKE EXPRESS
vessels.fact: First high-speed car ferry on the Great Lakes — 192 ft, 2004
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HSC_Lake_Express — CORRECTED: it was only 'one of the first' high-speed auto ferries built in the US, but 'the first high-speed auto ferry to see service on the Great Lakes'. 191 ft 7 in, Austal USA, service began 1 Jun 2004.

vessels.name: MANITOULIN
vessels.fact: Lower Lakes self-unloader — 1991 hull, 663 ft, 27,550 tons
vessels.source: VesselFinder (self-discharging bulk carrier, built 1991, 202 m, 27,550 DWT); en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lower_Lakes_Towing (fleet member). The 'new bow welded to an old tanker's stern in 2015' story could NOT be sourced from any reachable reference and was dropped.

vessels.name: ALGOMA INNOVATOR
vessels.fact: Algoma River Class self-unloader, 2018 — 24,454 DWT
vessels.source: algonet.com/fleet/algoma-innovator/ — CORRECTED build year to 2018 (Algoma lists built and delivered 2018, not 2017); River Class, 24,454 MT DWT, Canadian flag. Forward-mounted boom claim and 650 ft length could not be verified and were dropped.

vessels.name: USEPA LAKE GUARDIAN
vessels.fact: Largest research vessel on the Great Lakes — 180 ft floating lab
vessels.source: epa.gov/great-lakes-monitoring/lake-guardian — EPA states it is 'the largest research vessel operating on the Great Lakes', 180 feet, 959 gross tons. Build year not stated on the page; left as unknown.

vessels.name: STRANDJA
vessels.fact: Ex-Federal Yangtze — 610 ft saltie built in 2010
vessels.source: VesselFinder IMO 9564140 (bulk carrier, built 2010, 186 m, Malta flag, ex-FEDERAL YANGTZE, ex-EASTWIND YORK). Bulgarian operator and mountain-range naming could NOT be verified (Navibulgar site unreachable) and were dropped.

vessels.name: DIRK S. VANENKEVORT
vessels.fact: Built from leftover steel of the Joseph H. Thompson conversion
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SS_Marine_Robin — CORRECTED: the tug was 'constructed from leftover steel from the barge conversion', NOT from the cut-off stern; renamed from Joseph H. Thompson Jr. during a Donjon (Erie, PA) refit. Build year 1990 and 125 ft length could not be confirmed and were zeroed.

vessels.name: SAM LAUD
vessels.fact: Bay Shipbuilding 1975 — hauls 24,300 tons a trip
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MV_Sam_Laud — CORRECTED: launched 1975 and in service April 1975, not 1974; the 'side-launched' detail is not stated by any source found. 634 ft 10 in, Bay Shipbuilding Sturgeon Bay, 24,300 tons at midsummer draft (confirmed).

vessels.name: FEDERAL FRASER
vessels.fact: Built 2021 — 656 ft, 34,492 tons deadweight
vessels.source: VesselFinder IMO 9866744 (bulk carrier, built 2021, 199.98 m, 34,492 DWT, Marshall Islands flag). Japanese shipyard (Oshima) could not be independently confirmed — fednav.com returned 403 — so that detail was dropped.

vessels.name: PATRICIAHOEY
vessels.fact: Built 1949 · 2,000 hp tug still working the Detroit River
vessels.source: tugboatinformation.com/tug.cfm?id=5909, gltugs.wordpress.com/patricia-hoey/

vessels.name: STENHEIM
vessels.fact: Portuguese-flagged chemical tanker, 472 ft, built 2003
vessels.source: vesselfinder.com/vessels/details/9261114 (flag Portugal/Madeira, 144 m, 2003)

vessels.name: CITY OF ALGONAC
vessels.fact: Crosses the US–Canada border in about 10 minutes
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Walpole–Algonac_Ferry (built 1990, 8-10 min crossing)

vessels.name: GL OSTRANDER
vessels.fact: Tug + barge Integrity = 544-ft cement-hauling combo
vessels.source: gltugs.wordpress.com/g-l-ostrander/, tugboatinformation.com/tug.cfm?id=1430

vessels.name: RT HON PAUL J MARTIN
vessels.fact: Built 1973 as H.M. Griffith · 740-ft self-unloader
vessels.source: cslships.com/vessel/rt-hon-paul-j-martin/ (225.50 m = 740 ft; 738 was wrong), greatlakesships.wordpress.com/h-m-griffith/

vessels.name: MESABI MINER
vessels.fact: 1,004 ft — built in three pieces at two shipyards
vessels.source: interlake-steamship.com, doorcountypulse.com (midbody Toledo, assembled Lorain, American Shipbuilding)

vessels.name: EVANS SPIRIT
vessels.fact: Dutch-built 2007 · designed for shallow-draft Lakes ports
vessels.source: mckeil.com/our-fleet/evans-spirit/ (bulk/breakbulk, shallow draft — aluminum-slab claim NOT verifiable, removed); built Niestern Sander, Delfzijl NL

vessels.name: BAIE COMEAU
vessels.fact: Built in China, 2013 — sailed across the Pacific to the Lakes
vessels.source: boatnerd.com/pictures/fleet/BaieComeau.htm (Chengxi Shipyard, delivered 20 Jun 2013, departed China 30 Jun)

vessels.name: ALGOCANADA
vessels.fact: Built 2008 · 426-ft tanker hauling petroleum on the Lakes
vessels.source: vesselfinder.com/vessels/details/9378591 (129.84 m = 426 ft, 2008, Canadian-flag chem/oil products tanker)

vessels.name: WALTER J MCCARTHY
vessels.fact: First 1,000-footer built at Bay Shipbuilding, 1977
vessels.source: doorcountypulse.com (superlative explicitly confirmed), en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MV_Walter_J._McCarthy_Jr.

vessels.name: THUNDER BAY
vessels.fact: CSL Trillium-class laker — built in China, delivered 2013
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trillium-class_freighter (Chengxi Shipyard, Jiangyin; delivered 16 May 2013), cslships.com/vessel/thunder-bay/ (225.50 m)

vessels.name: MARK W. BARKER
vessels.fact: First new US-built laker in nearly 40 years · 639 ft
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MV_Mark_W._Barker; interlake-steamship.com — first US-flagged Great Lakes bulk carrier built since 1983

vessels.name: KAYE E BARKER
vessels.fact: 1952 · first lake freighter built entirely in drydock
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MV_Kaye_E._Barker — built as SS Edward B. Greene, first laker built from keel up in drydock (Toledo)

vessels.name: G3 MARQUIS
vessels.fact: 740-ft Equinox-class grain hauler, built 2014
vessels.source: boatnerd.com/pictures/fleet/G3Marquis.htm; en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Equinox-class_freighter — launched 8 Jul 2014, 740x78

vessels.name: JOSEPH L BLOCK
vessels.fact: 1976 · first diesel ship in the Inland Steel fleet
vessels.source: greatlakesvesselhistory.com; greatlakesships.wordpress.com/joseph-l-block — christened 29 Jun 1976, first diesel in Inland Steel fleet

vessels.name: JOHN G MUNSON
vessels.fact: World's largest self-unloader when built in 1952
vessels.source: greatlakesvesselhistory.com Munson John G. 2 — explicitly the largest self-unloader in the world when built; lengthened 1976

vessels.name: EXEBORG
vessels.fact: Dutch saltwater trader — built 2013 · 474 ft
vessels.source: vesselfinder.com IMO 9650482 — 144.56 m (474 ft), Netherlands flag, general cargo, built 2013. Length corrected from 476 to 474.

vessels.name: PETIT FORTE
vessels.fact: Canadian pusher tug working the lakes since 1969
vessels.source: vesselfinder.com IMO 6826119 — pusher tug, built 1969, Canada flag. CORRECTED: original claim that it pushes the barge 'St. Marys Cement' could NOT be verified and was removed.

vessels.name: ALPENA
vessels.fact: Built 1942 — oldest active freighter on the Great Lakes
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SS_Alpena_(1942); glc.org/dailynews/20250829-oldest-freighter — 519 ft after 1989 shortening

vessels.name: CSL TADOUSSAC
vessels.fact: Last CSL laker built with the classic bow bridge
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CSL_Tadoussac — 'the last freighter built for CSL in the traditional two superstructure design, which puts her bridge up in the ship's bow'

vessels.name: AMERICAN MARINER
vessels.fact: 1980 self-unloader with a 250-ft stern boom · 730 ft
vessels.source: greatlakesships.wordpress.com/american-mariner — 730 ft LOA, 1980, aft-mounted 250-ft boom. CORRECTED: the '6,000 tons an hour' rate is NOT published for this vessel (that figure belongs to Joseph L. Block) and was removed.

vessels.name: AMERICAN PATRIOT
vessels.fact: New in 2025 — sails the first all-U.S. Great Lakes cruises
vessels.source: americancruiselines.com press release; travelweekly.com — 'first all-U.S.' rests on operator's own claim, echoed by trade press; length not verified

vessels.name: CSL ST-LAURENT
vessels.fact: Trillium Class laker built in China — 740 ft long
vessels.source: Wikipedia Trillium-class freighter (225.5 m / 739 ft 10 in, Yangfan Shipyard, Zhoushan, China, 2014); cslships.com spec sheet

vessels.name: CSL LAURENTIEN
vessels.fact: Sailing since 1977 — rebuilt as a self-unloader in 2001
vessels.source: cslships.com/vessel/csl-laurentien — built 1977, converted to self-unloader 2001, LOA 225.48 m, ex-Louis R. Desmarais. NOTE: original 'whole new forebody' wording dropped — CSL documents a self-unloader conversion, not a forebody swap

vessels.name: DOROTHY ANN
vessels.fact: 124-ft Z-drive tug — pushing barge Pathfinder since 1999
vessels.source: vesselfinder.com (pusher tug, 1999, 38 m = 124 ft); Wikipedia Interlake Steamship Company + J. L. Mauthe. CORRECTED: barge Pathfinder is 647 ft, not 700 ft, and the pair is an ARTICULATED tug-barge, not a rigid unit — both errors removed

vessels.name: CALUMET
vessels.fact: Built 1973 as William R. Roesch — still hauling at 630 ft
vessels.source: Wikipedia Calumet (1973 ship) — 630 ft, built 1973 by American Ship Building, Lorain OH, ex-William R. Roesch, still in service

vessels.name: FEDERAL BISCAY
vessels.fact: Ocean-going salty — 656 ft, 34,500 tons deadweight
vessels.source: vesselfinder.com IMO 9697856 — 2015, 199.98 m (656 ft), 34,564 DWT. NOTE: 'built in Japan' removed, could not be confirmed

vessels.name: AMERICAN CENTURY
vessels.fact: A 1,000-footer — carries 80,900 tons of ore or coal
vessels.source: Wikipedia MV American Century — 1,000 ft, built 1981 Bay Shipbuilding, 80,900 tons at midsummer draft, ex-Columbia Star

vessels.name: KAREN ANDRIE
vessels.fact: Pusher tug working the Lakes since 1965 — 112 ft
vessels.source: vesselfinder.com — pusher tug, built 1965, 34 m (112 ft), 458 GT, in service. CORRECTED: 'Texas-built' and the barge Endeavour pairing could not be confirmed and were removed

vessels.name: WILFRED SYKES
vessels.fact: Queen of the Lakes 1949-52 — first streamlined laker
vessels.source: Wikipedia Queen of the Lakes — held title 28 Jun 1949 to Nov 1952, succeeded by Joseph H. Thompson, noted as first streamlined design. NOTE: 'still sailing after 75 years' removed — current operating status not confirmed

vessels.name: BBC KOLDING
vessels.fact: Brand-new general cargo ship — launched in 2026
vessels.source: vesselfinder.com IMO 9964479 — general cargo, built 2026, 149 m (489 ft). NOTE: 'one of the newest on the Lakes' was an unverifiable inference and was removed

vessels.name: ALGOSOLIS
vessels.fact: Product tanker that joined Algoma's Canadian fleet in 2024
vessels.source: algonet.com/ship/algosolis (built 2009, delivered to Algoma 2024, 16,544 DWT); vesselfinder.com IMO 9409261 = 144.1 m LOA, built 2009, Jiangnan Shipyard, Algoma Tankers Ltd

vessels.name: CLYDE S. VANENKEVORT
vessels.fact: Tug that pushes the 740-ft self-unloading barge Erie Trader
vessels.source: gltugs.wordpress.com/clyde-s-vanenkevort — built 2011 by DonJon Shipbuilding, Erie PA as KEN BOOTHE SR., 135 ft 4 in, renamed 2017; vtbarge.com fleet page + NTSB MAB1912 confirm Erie Trader 740 ft, 2012. CORRECTED: input said built 2008 / 130 ft

vessels.name: VERILA
vessels.fact: Handysize bulk carrier, Malta-flagged, brand new in 2022
vessels.source: vesselfinder.com vessel search VERILA — bulk carrier, built 2022, 32,165 DWT, 190 x 24 m; vesseltracker.com IMO 9905734, MMSI 229656000 / callsign 9HA5549 = Malta flag. CORRECTED: could NOT verify 'Bulgarian' flag (Malta-flagged) or the ice-class / 'built for winter Seaway runs' claim — Seaway closes in winter, so that claim was dropped

vessels.name: PEARL MIST
vessels.fact: Finished in 2010 — lawsuits delayed her debut until 2014
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pearl_Mist — completed June 2010 at Halifax Shipyards, 99.1 m (325 ft 2 in), 210 passengers, legal battles settled after 2013 appeals ruling, maiden voyage June 2014

vessels.name: ALGOMA STRONGFIELD
vessels.fact: 740 ft — the biggest that fits the Welland Canal locks
vessels.source: algonet.com/ship/algoma-strongfield — built 2017, class listed as 'Seaway Max', 30,303 DWT; 740 ft is the maximum permitted Seaway/Welland lock length (225.5 m)

vessels.name: SAMUEL DE CHAMPLAIN
vessels.fact: Built 1976 as an ocean tug — now pushes cement on the Lakes
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Samuel_de_Champlain_(tugboat) — built 1976 by Mangone Shipbuilding, Houston as MUSKETEER FURY for a Norwegian owner (ocean tug), 140 ft; bought by Lafarge 2006, paired with 460-ft cement barge INNOVATION

vessels.name: ARTHUR M ANDERSON
vessels.fact: Last ship to speak with the Edmund Fitzgerald, 1975
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SS_Arthur_M._Anderson — launched 1952, originally 647 ft, lengthened to 767 ft winter 1974-75; explicitly 'the last ship to be in contact with SS Edmund Fitzgerald before she sank on November 10, 1975' and first rescue vessel on scene

vessels.name: CASON J CALLAWAY
vessels.fact: Built 1952 · stretched 120 ft longer in 1974
vessels.source: greatlakesships.wordpress.com/cason-j-callaway — built 1952, 'Lengthened by 120', Fraser Shipyards, Superior, Wisconsin, 1974', 647 ft to 767 ft

vessels.name: PHILIP R CLARKE
vessels.fact: First of the eight AAA-class lake freighters, built 1952
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Great_Lakes_Fleet — 'First vessel of the AAA Class ore carriers'; en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SS_Arthur_M._Anderson calls Anderson 'second of eight of the AAA class', confirming the class size of 8. REWORDED: original said 'eight 1952 AAA class' but the class was built 1952-53, not all in 1952

vessels.name: STEWART J CORT
vessels.fact: First 1,000-footer on the Lakes — wears a big #1 aft
vessels.source: interlake-steamship.com/our-fleet/m-v-stewart-j-cort — 'the first 1000-foot vessel on the Great Lakes when she entered service for Bethlehem Steel in 1972'; 1,000 x 105 ft; 'She has a #1 painted on her aft house, a nod to her legacy of being the first thousand footer on the Lakes'

vessels.name: ROGER BLOUGH
vessels.fact: 858 ft — a construction fire delayed her 1972 debut a year
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MV_Roger_Blough — 858 ft, American Ship Building, Lorain OH; June 24, 1971 engine room fire killed four, pushed the July 1971 launch to service entry June 1972. CAUTION for live use: laid up at Conneaut, OH since Oct 2022 — an AIS contact near the Straits under this name is likely spurious

vessels.name: JAMES R BARKER
vessels.fact: First 1,000-footer built entirely on the Great Lakes
vessels.source: interlake-steamship.com (owner), duluth-ships wiki, Duluth News Tribune — superlative confirmed: first 1,000-ft class vessel built entirely on the Lakes, American Ship Building, Lorain OH, 1976

vessels.name: MICHIPICOTEN
vessels.fact: Built 1952 · survived a 13-ft hull crack in 2024
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Michipicoten_(1952_ship), NOAA IncidentNews #10799, gcaptain.com — LOA corrected from 698 to 683 ft (Wikipedia, post-1957 lengthening); USCG press used a rounded 690 ft

vessels.name: H LEE WHITE
vessels.fact: Built 1974 · carries 35,400 tons at midsummer draft
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MV_H._Lee_White, greatlakesships.wordpress.com — REPLACED prior fact: the '6,000 tons per hour' unload rate appears only on fan wikis, not in Wikipedia or Shipwatcher specs; could not confirm, so dropped

vessels.name: BUFFALO
vessels.fact: Built 1978 · sails today as the Algoma Buffalo
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MV_Buffalo — built 1978 Bay Shipbuilding, 634 ft 10 in LOA, bought by Algoma late 2017 and renamed Algoma Buffalo in 2018; a live 'BUFFALO' AIS hit is likely stale

vessels.name: ADAM E CORNELIUS
vessels.fact: Built 1973 · renamed Algoma Compass in 2018
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Algoma_Compass — built 1973, originally Roger M. Kyes, renamed Adam E. Cornelius 1989, Algoma Compass 2018; LOA 680 ft 1 in

vessels.name: ALGOMA CONVEYOR
vessels.fact: Its hull was bought at auction after the yard went bust
vessels.source: greatlakesships.wordpress.com/algoma-conveyor — hull started at Nantong Mingde (bankrupt), acquired by Algoma at auction, completed at Yangzijiang; 740 ft LOA

vessels.name: ALGOMA SAULT
vessels.fact: 740 ft — the maximum length the Seaway locks allow
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Algoma_Sault — 740 ft LOA, Equinox-class, seawaymax. REWORDED: original 'the longest that fits' implied uniqueness; many lakers share the 740 ft Seaway max, so the superlative was softened to the limit itself

vessels.name: TIM S DOOL
vessels.fact: Built 1967 · widened in 1996, re-engined in 2007
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tim_S._Dool — launched 1967 as Senneville, beam widened at Port Weller Dry Docks 1996, re-engined with MaK 8M43C in 2007; 730 ft LOA

vessels.name: MANITOWOC
vessels.fact: Built 1973 · sailed first as the Paul Thayer
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Ship_Building_Company — CORRECTED: 'four different names' is wrong. Sources confirm only three: Paul Thayer (1973), Earl W. Oglebay (1994, Oglebay Norton), Manitowoc (2008, Lower Lakes). No source found for a separate 'Earl W.' name

vessels.name: VICTORY I
vessels.fact: Small-ship cruiser — visits all five Great Lakes, 202 guests
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Victory_I; victorycruiselines.com/2026-voyage-calendar-2/

vessels.name: ALGOMA BUFFALO
vessels.fact: 250-ft boom unloads 5,900 tons of cargo every hour
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MV_Buffalo; algonet.com Algoma Buffalo marketing sheet

vessels.name: ALGOMA BEAR
vessels.fact: Built 2024 — 740 ft, the biggest the Seaway locks allow
vessels.source: algonet.com/ship/algoma-bear/; vesselfinder.com IMO 9619309

vessels.name: EDWARD L RYERSON
vessels.fact: Last steam-powered freighter ever built on the Lakes
vessels.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SS_Edward_L._Ryerson; greatlakeships.org/3706345/data

vessels.name: BADGER
vessels.fact: Last coal-fired passenger steamship in the US, built 1953
vessels.source: ssbadger.com/history/; en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SS_Badger

vessels.name: CGC BUCKTHORN
vessels.fact: Oldest Coast Guard cutter on the Great Lakes — built 1963
vessels.source: atlanticarea.uscg.mil District 9 BUCKTHORN History; greatlakesnow.org 2020 'Oldest Coast Guard cutter'

vessels.name: KATHY MCKEIL
vessels.fact: Ex-tanker Algosea — rebuilt as a dry bulk carrier in 2025
vessels.source: mckeil.com/our-fleet/kathy-mckeil/ (1998/2025, 588'4", dry bulk); vesselfinder.com IMO 9127198 (ex-Algosea to Nov 2023)

vessels.name: ONTARIO VENTURE
vessels.fact: A 2004 tanker stern joined to a brand-new self-unloading bow
vessels.source: mckeil.com/our-fleet/ontario-venture/ (2004/2025, 679'10", self-unloading bulk carrier)

vessels.name: PRESQUE ISLE
vessels.fact: The only 1,000-footer that's a tug and barge locked together
vessels.source: boatnerd.com/pictures/fleet/prsqisle.htm; gltugs.wordpress.com/presque-isle/ (only integrated tug on the Great Lakes)

vessels.name: R/V LAKE EXPLORER II
vessels.fact: An EPA floating lab sampling Great Lakes water quality
vessels.source: EPA Great Lakes monitoring research vessel program; build year 1966 and 90-ft length could NOT be confirmed, so omitted

vessels.name: CSL ASSINIBOINE
vessels.fact: Built 1977 — a 728-ft self-unloader still working the Lakes
vessels.source: vesselfinder.com IMO 7413218 (1977, 222m = 728ft, self-discharging bulk carrier). Original cited URLs were dead; 2005 forebody-splice claim unverifiable, removed

vessels.name: KAMINISTIQUA
vessels.fact: Built 1983, sailed as Voyageur Pioneer until 2008
vessels.source: vesselfinder.com IMO 8119285 (1983, 222.54m = 730ft, ex-VOYAGEUR PIONEER pre-2008). Clyde/Scotland build claim unverifiable, removed

vessels.name: CARIBOU ISLE
vessels.fact: Canadian Coast Guard small buoy tender
vessels.source: Wikipedia, List of equipment of the Canadian Coast Guard — lists CCGS Caribou Isle as small buoy tender. 2.5-ton crane claim, 75 ft and 1985 could not be verified.

vessels.name: WELLAND
vessels.fact: Russel Brothers tug of 1954 — still docking ships today
vessels.source: gltugs.wordpress.com/welland/ — built 1954 Russel Brothers, Owen Sound; 86 ft; ship-docking at Sarnia since 2022. Original entry said 80 ft and 'built for the Seaway' — it was built for the Dept of Transport, Seaway Authority came later.

vessels.name: AMERICAN SPIRIT
vessels.fact: 1,004 feet — launched 1978 as the George A. Stinson
vessels.source: Wikipedia 'Queen of the Lakes' — George A. Stinson, 1,004 ft, launched 15 July 1978, renamed American Spirit. Confirmed by Wikipedia 'American Steamship Company' fleet list.

vessels.name: CAROLINE MCKEE
vessels.fact: Built 1972 in Louisiana — 5,120 hp of barge-pushing muscle
vessels.source: gltugs.wordpress.com/caroline-mckee/ — built 1972 by Main Iron Works, Houma LA; 5,120 bhp (two ALCO 16-251E); 129 ft (entry said 135 ft — corrected); pushes cement barge Commander for Port City Marine.

vessels.name: VICTORY II
vessels.fact: Great Lakes cruise ship — sailed as Ocean Navigator
vessels.source: Wikipedia 'American Queen' — AQSC acquired Victory Cruise Lines (Victory I and Victory II) in Jan 2019; reintroduced as Ocean Voyager and Ocean Navigator respectively. The '190 guests' figure and 'visits all five Lakes' claim could not be verified (sister ship Victory I is 202-210), so both were dropped.

vessels.name: JOE DERBAS
vessels.fact: Built 1973 — renamed in 2023 after four earlier names
vessels.source: gltugs.wordpress.com/joe-derbas/ — built 1973 by Main Iron Works, Houma LA; prior names National Freedom, Compass Freedom, Mike Marlier, Al Weaver (exactly four); renamed by Illinois & Michigan Oil around 2023.

vessels.name: KIOWA
vessels.fact: Built 1965 as the Will Lewis — still pushing aggregate
vessels.source: gltugs.wordpress.com/kiowa/ — built 1965 as Will Lewis; 76 ft; Hanson Material Service, moves aggregate barges in the Chicago area.

vessels.name: OMSTEADER
vessels.fact: 1981 Hurley-built fish tug — 80 ft of classic Lake Erie trawler
vessels.source: https://evercleverwilson.com/fish-tugs-project/

vessels.name: ALBERT
vessels.fact: Four names since 1979 — now pushes the tank barge Margaret
vessels.source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Albert_(tugboat)

vessels.name: EVERLAST
vessels.fact: Built in Japan in 1976 — now pushes asphalt barge Norman McLeod
vessels.source: https://gltugs.wordpress.com/everlast/

vessels.name: EDGAR B SPEER
vessels.fact: 1,004 ft, 1980 — tied for 2nd-longest on the Great Lakes
vessels.source: Wikipedia MV Edgar B. Speer / Great Lakes 1000-footer length lists

vessels.name: AMERICAN INTEGRITY
vessels.fact: 1,000-footer from 1978 — unloads 10,000 tons per hour
vessels.source: American Steamship Co. fleet page / Wikipedia MV American Integrity

vessels.name: CSL NIAGARA
vessels.fact: 1972 stern, 1999 bow — half this ship is 27 years newer
vessels.source: Boatnerd CSL Niagara / Shipwatcher News J.W. McGiffin

vessels.name: BAIE ST PAUL
vessels.fact: First of CSL's Trillium class, 2012 — 740 ft of Seaway max
vessels.source: Wikipedia Baie St. Paul (2012 ship) / Trillium-class freighter / CSL

vessels.name: WHITEFISH BAY
vessels.fact: Trillium laker, 2013 — swings a 259-foot unloading boom
vessels.source: CSL official vessel page (boom 79.00 m, built 2013)

vessels.name: SPRUCEGLEN
vessels.fact: 1983 Seawaymax bulk carrier — gearless, 34,150 tonnes dwt
vessels.source: CSL official vessel page (222.17 m LOA, 34,150 t dwt, gearless)

vessels.name: ATLANTIC HURON
vessels.fact: Built 1984, converted to a self-unloader in 1989
vessels.source: CSL Atlantic Huron technical specification sheet (736'6")

vessels.name: ALGOMA HARVESTER
vessels.fact: Equinox class, 2014 — 45% more fuel-efficient by design
vessels.source: Wikipedia Equinox-class freighter / Algoma Central fleet page

vessels.name: ALGOMA INTEGRITY
vessels.fact: 2009 handysize bulk carrier — 46,293 tonnes deadweight
vessels.source: Algoma Central official fleet page

vessels.name: ALGOMA MARINER
vessels.fact: 2011 — first new Canadian-flag ship on the Lakes in 25 years
vessels.source: Wikipedia MV Algoma Mariner / Algoma Central fleet page

vessels.name: ALGOMA VICTORY
vessels.fact: Panamax bulk carrier, 1999 — 66,188 tonnes deadweight
vessels.source: Algoma Central official fleet page

vessels.name: ALGOMA VERITY
vessels.fact: Ocean-going self-unloader, ex-Alice Oldendorff, built 2000
vessels.source: VesselFinder IMO 9183776 (190m LOA, ex ALICE OLDENDORFF, ex NING RUI YANG); offshore-energy.biz Algoma/Oldendorff acquisition

vessels.name: ALGOTERRA
vessels.fact: 473-ft product tanker — carries refined fuel, not iron ore
vessels.source: algonet.com Algoterra spec sheet (LOA 144.18 m = 473 ft, 16,512 dwt); Marine Log 2019 delivery

vessels.name: JOSEPH H THOMPSON
vessels.fact: WWII troopship Marine Robin of 1944 — now a lakes barge
vessels.source: Wikipedia SS Marine Robin (1943); greatlakesvesselhistory.com. D-DAY CLAIM REMOVED — Wikipedia notes she is absent from the Normandy Operation Plan and the American Merchant Marine at War invasion list; she was in the Mediterranean for Operation Dragoon.

vessels.name: GREAT LAKES TRADER
vessels.fact: 740-ft barge of 2000, built in two halves in Mississippi
vessels.source: vtbarge.com fleet page (740 ft LOA, built 2000); greatlakesships.wordpress.com. SUPERLATIVE REMOVED — two fleetmates are also 740 ft.

vessels.name: ERIE TRADER
vessels.fact: 740-ft barge of 2012 — sailed as Lakes Contender till 2017
vessels.source: vtbarge.com fleet page (740 ft, built 2012); greatlakesships.wordpress.com Great Lakes Trader page confirms ex-Lakes Contender

vessels.name: SAGINAW
vessels.fact: Working since 1953 — launched as the John J. Boland
vessels.source: Wikipedia MV Saginaw — built 1953, launched as John J. Boland, 195.0 m (639 ft 9 in), in active service

vessels.name: LEWIS J KUBER
vessels.fact: Built 1952 — engine cut out in 2006 to make her a barge
vessels.source: greatlakesships.wordpress.com/sparrows-point — built 1952, engine removed and stern notched at Erie Marine in 2006, renamed Lewis J. Kuber, paired with tug Olive L. Moore

vessels.name: PATHFINDER
vessels.fact: Steamer J.L. Mauthe of 1952, reborn as a barge in 1998
vessels.source: Wikipedia J. L. Mauthe — built 1952 (input said 1953, CORRECTED), converted to self-unloading barge and renamed Pathfinder in 1998

vessels.name: ST MARYS CHALLENGER
vessels.fact: Hull dates to 1906 — cut down to a cement barge in 2014
vessels.source: Wikipedia SS St. Marys Challenger; professionalmariner.com 2014. SUPERLATIVE SOFTENED — she was the oldest operating self-propelled laker, a title that lapsed at conversion. NOTE: 552 ft is as-built; Wikipedia gives 527 ft as the shortened barge length.

vessels.name: ST MARYS CONQUEST
vessels.fact: Born 1937 as the oil tanker Red Crown — now hauls cement
vessels.source: greatlakesships.wordpress.com/red-crown — built 1937 by Manitowoc for Standard Oil of Indiana as tanker Red Crown; barge conversion 1987. LENGTH CORRECTED: 465 ft was as-built; 437 ft 6 in after the 1992 rebuild.

vessels.name: USCGC MACKINAW
vessels.fact: Great Lakes heavy icebreaker — replaced the 1944 Mackinaw
vessels.source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/USCGC_Mackinaw_(WLBB-30)

vessels.name: CGC HOLLYHOCK
vessels.fact: 225-ft buoy tender and icebreaker — left the Lakes in 2023
vessels.source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/USCGC_Hollyhock_(WLB-214)

vessels.name: CGC ALDER
vessels.fact: The last Juniper-class buoy tender ever built — launched 2004
vessels.source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/USCGC_Alder_(WLB-216)

vessels.name: CGC BISCAYNE BAY
vessels.fact: Homeported at St. Ignace since 1979 — the bridge's own icebreaker
vessels.source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/USCGC_Biscayne_Bay

vessels.name: CGC NEAH BAY
vessels.fact: Blows air bubbles under her hull to slip through thick ice
vessels.source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/USCGC_Neah_Bay

vessels.name: CGC KATMAI BAY
vessels.fact: Lead ship of the Bay class — first of nine, Soo-based since 1979
vessels.source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/USCGC_Katmai_Bay

vessels.name: CGC MOBILE BAY
vessels.fact: Breaks 27 inches of ice at 3 knots — Sturgeon Bay's icebreaker
vessels.source: https://www.atlanticarea.uscg.mil/Our-Organization/District-9/Ninth-District-Staff/Prevention-Division/Cutters/MOBILE-BAY/Characteristics/

vessels.name: CGC BRISTOL BAY
vessels.fact: Sails with her own 120-ft buoy barge, mated back in 1991
vessels.source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/USCGC_Bristol_Bay

vessels.name: VIKING POLARIS
vessels.fact: Carries two six-passenger submarines — built 2022 for polar ice
vessels.source: https://www.vard.com/shipbuilding/references/viking-polaris

vessels.name: LE CHAMPLAIN
vessels.fact: Has 'Blue Eye' — a lounge with windows below the waterline
vessels.source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Le_Champlain

vessels.name: JOHN D LEITCH
vessels.fact: Sailing since 1967 — rebuilt in 2002 and renamed John D. Leitch
vessels.source: https://www.boatnerd.com/pictures/fleet/JohnDLeitch.htm

vessels.name: RADCLIFFE R LATIMER
vessels.fact: Rebuilt in China 2008-09 — sailed as Algobay until renamed 2012
vessels.source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radcliffe_R._Latimer — LENGTH CORRECTED: 730ft was the pre-rebuild figure; the 2009 China rebuild took LOA to 741ft 10in. Renamed 4 Oct 2012 for a former Algoma Central board chairman.

vessels.name: TIM MCKEIL
vessels.fact: Built in Japan in 1991 to serve Australian iron ore ports
vessels.source: https://gltugs.wordpress.com/tim-mckeil/ — built 1991 by Matsuura Tekko Zosen, Higashino, Japan as Pannawonica I for Cleveland Cliffs-Robe River Iron (Australia). LENGTH CORRECTED 105 -> 107ft 09in. Single-source.

vessels.name: JARRETT M
vessels.fact: Built 1945 in Owen Sound, Ontario as the tug Atomic
vessels.source: https://gltugs.wordpress.com/jarrett-m/ — built 1945 by Russel Brothers Ltd, Owen Sound, as Atomic; still active for McKeil. LENGTH CORRECTED 80.5 -> 82ft. Dropped '80 years on the towline' — it is 81 years as of 2026 and the number goes stale.

vessels.name: VICTORIOUS
vessels.fact: China-built 2009 ATB tug — 5,384 hp pushing heated asphalt
vessels.source: https://gltugs.wordpress.com/leo-a-mcarthur/ — verified: 2009 Penglai Bohai Shipyard, China; 5,384 bhp; 122ft; mated to heated asphalt barge John J. Carrick. CAVEAT: renamed Leo A. McArthur in 2017, so the AIS name is likely a stale static entry on the same hull — the fact remains true of that hull.

vessels.name: JOHN J CARRICK
vessels.fact: Heated asphalt barge — 70,000 barrels, built in China in 2009
vessels.source: https://professionalmariner.com/another-new-atb-for-great-lakes-service-tug-victorious-is-second-unit-for-mcasphalt/ — BUILD YEAR CORRECTED 2008 -> 2009 (Penglai Bohai Shipyard). Length filled in at ~410ft LOA. 70,000 bbl and heated asphalt (to 350F) confirmed.

vessels.name: MENOMINEE
vessels.fact: Built 1952, stretched 72 ft in 1958, cut down to a barge in 2006
vessels.source: https://greatlakesships.wordpress.com/sparrows-point/ — ex-Sparrows Point. Lengthened 72ft at American Shipbuilding South Chicago over winter 1957-58; barged at Erie Marine 2006. LENGTH CORRECTED 698 -> 616ft (barge alone); 728ft only when combined with tug Olive L. Moore.

vessels.name: UNDAUNTED
vessels.fact: WWII Navy tug, then Kings Point training ship, now a barge pusher
vessels.source: https://gltugs.wordpress.com/undaunted/ — BUILD YEAR CORRECTED 1944 -> 1943 (ATA-199, Gulfport Boiler & Welding Works, Port Arthur TX). Decommissioned 1948; to Kings Point 1963 as Kings Pointer; now paired with barge Pere Marquette 41. Year is single-source; Wikipedia ATA-199 page not independently re-checked.

vessels.name: PERE MARQUETTE 41
vessels.fact: Carried a million railcars in 47 years as a carferry
vessels.source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SS_City_of_Midland_41 — the million-railcars/47-years claim CHECKS OUT (carferry 1941-1988, ~1 million cars, 3.5 million miles). LENGTH CORRECTED 403 -> 406ft. Launched Sept 1940, completed March 1941; barged 1997.

vessels.name: G L OSTRANDER
vessels.fact: Built 1976 in Louisiana as the Andrew Martin — renamed 2004
vessels.source: https://gltugs.wordpress.com/g-l-ostrander/ — built 1976 by Halter Marine, Pierre Part LA; ex-Andrew Martin; renamed 2004; pushes cement barge Integrity. REMOVED 'named for a Lafarge executive' — the source does NOT state the namesake, so that was unverified. Length 140ft 02in.

vessels.name: INTEGRITY
vessels.fact: Cement barge built 1996 — 17,557 tons across eight holds
vessels.source: https://greatlakesships.wordpress.com/integrity/ — every element confirmed exactly: 1996, 460ft barge alone (544ft with tug), 17,557 tons, 8 holds, self-unloading cement carrier. No changes needed.

vessels.name: JOYCE L VANENKEVORT
vessels.fact: Just 135 ft of tug — pushes the 740-ft barge Great Lakes Trader
vessels.source: VERIFIED. gltugs.wordpress.com/joyce-l-vanenkevort/ gives LOA 135'04", built 1998 by Bay Shipbuilding; Great Lakes Trader barge confirmed at 740 ft LOA (vtbarge.com/vtbourfleet, greatlakesships.wordpress.com/great-lakes-trader/).

vessels.name: GREAT REPUBLIC
vessels.fact: Twin props and eight rudders — built to twist up the Cuyahoga
vessels.source: CORRECTED — original said 'four rudders'; boatnerd.com/vessel-info/great-lakes-fleet/great-republic/ documents an EIGHT-rudder system (two aft plus two flanking rudders per Kort-nozzled propeller). Length 634'10", launched 1981 at Bay Shipbuilding, purpose-built for the Cuyahoga River.

vessels.name: KEN BOOTHE SR
vessels.fact: 135-ft, 10,700-hp tug built at Erie, PA to push a 740-ft barge
vessels.source: CORRECTED — the original superlative was mis-attributed: erieshipnews.blogspot.com applies 'first new bulk cargo vessel built in Erie in almost forty years' to the BARGE Lakes Contender, not to this tug. Replaced with tug specs verified in the same article (135 ft, 10,700 hp, Donjon Shipbuilding, Erie PA; christened 4/10/2012, so built year set to 2012 not 2011). CAUTION: this hull was reportedly renamed CLYDE S. VANENKEVORT after the VanEnkevort acquisition, so an AIS contact under the old name may be stale or a different vessel.

vessels.name: LAKES CONTENDER
vessels.fact: 740-ft self-unloading barge with no engines of its own
vessels.source: CORRECTED — dropped the disputed tonnage: erieshipnews.blogspot.com says 34,000 tons while greatlakesships.wordpress.com/lakes-contender/ says 38,516 tons, so no figure is safe to broadcast. The 740-ft length, 2012 build at Donjon Shipbuilding (Erie, PA) and the engineless articulated-barge design (Hydraconn ATB connection, pushed by a tug) are confirmed by both sources.

vessels.name: MARK W BARKER
vessels.fact: First new U.S.-built Great Lakes freighter since 1983
vessels.source: Interlake Steamship / Wikipedia / Professional Mariner

general: Ships get 155 feet of clearance under the bridge's center span
The navigation channel under the bridge is 3,000 feet wide
Clearance drops to 135 feet at the navigation channel's edges
The only natural water link from Lake Michigan to Lake Huron
The straits reach 295 feet deep at their deepest point
Soo Locks close Jan 15 and reopen March 25 every year
Over 70 million tons of cargo pass the Soo Locks each year
Typical cargo: iron ore, limestone, coal, cement and grain
Minnesota iron ore passes here bound for Gary, Indiana steel
Seaway locks cap ships at 740 feet — 1,000-footers never leave
The Poe Lock, 1,200 by 110 feet, is the only one that fits them
Icebreaker Mackinaw keeps a winter lane open through the ice
The Paul R. Tregurtha is the longest laker at 1,013.5 feet
The Tregurtha can unload 10,000 tons of iron ore per hour
Self-unloading booms empty these ships with no shore cranes
Pilothouse forward, engines aft — a look set by the 1869 R.J. Hackett
Deck hatches sit 24 feet apart, matching 1800s ore dock chutes
Even the biggest lakers top out around 15 knots
The bridge towers rise 552 feet above the water
The bridge's tower foundations reach 210 feet below the water
Longest suspension bridge between anchorages in the Americas
Currents here reverse direction about once every 1.5 days
Wind can push 80,000 cubic meters of water per second through
The freighter Cedarville sank here in 1965 fog, killing 10
Icebreaker Mackinaw, 240 feet long, is based in Cheboygan
Mackinaw cuts 32 inches of ice at 3 knots, 42 by riding on top
Great Lakes ice cover hit a record 94.7% back in 1979
